findings.claim: logreg_raw is a valid baseline for testing PLE's contribution to capturing a non-monotonic fraud-vs-amount relationship.
findings.failure_mechanism: LogisticRegression on a raw log-amount scalar cannot represent a U-shape (monotone only). The +0.559 delta measures non-linear-encoding vs an incapable baseline, not PLE vs the best raw encoding. A logreg_quadratic (log_amount + log_amount^2) would represent the U-shape directly. If it matches logreg_ple, PLE's specific contribution is zero.
findings.evidence_test: Add logreg_quadratic: LR on [log_amount, log_amount^2, onehot_category, G]. Within-CI of logreg_ple => PLE adds nothing beyond a polynomial basis.

findings.claim: PLE bins fit on raw dollar amounts are aligned with the U-shape, which is defined in standardized log-amount space.
findings.failure_mechanism: Labels use la_std (log-amount space); PLE bins are quantiles on raw amount. The natural encoding to pair with a log-amount U-shape is PLE on log_amount. Applying PLE to raw amount introduces a granularity/mechanism mismatch that may inflate or distort PLE's apparent performance.
findings.evidence_test: Add logreg_ple_log (PLE on log_amount). Compare to logreg_ple (PLE on raw amount).

findings.claim: Temporal split with train-fit PLE edges validly simulates PLE in the reference model under drift.
findings.failure_mechanism: Amounts drift up over time; train-fit PLE edges mean test high-amounts saturate the top bin (->1.0), losing resolution on the right arm of the U-shape (the fraud-relevant region) exactly in test.
findings.evidence_test: Compare PLE bin variance train vs test; or temporal vs random-split PR-AUC for logreg_ple.

findings.claim: Global la_std standardization in label generation is consistent with a real temporal deployment.
findings.failure_mechanism: la_std uses full-dataset (train+test) mean/std, so the U-shape's safe-middle is anchored to a distribution blending future data; PLE bins fit on train are calibrated to a different center.
findings.evidence_test: Regenerate labels with train-only la_std stats; rerun. Delta>0.05 => material distortion.

findings.claim: hgb_raw - logreg_ple (+0.016) is a valid 'interaction gap proxy'.
findings.failure_mechanism: logreg_ple lacks interaction terms, so the gap mixes PLE contribution with GBDT interaction modeling. Correct measurement is hgb_raw vs hgb_ple. README acknowledges hgb_ple deferred but still labels the current gap an interaction proxy (the mischaracterization).

findings.claim: PR-AUC/precision@k are comparable despite class_weight asymmetry (logreg balanced, hgb not).
findings.failure_mechanism: class_weight='balanced' shifts the logreg score distribution toward fraud, inflating precision@k vs an uncalibrated hgb. Comparison mixes encoding quality with imbalance strategy.
findings.evidence_test: Harmonize weighting across arms (or use calibrated metrics); recheck whether logreg_ple's edge persists.

findings.claim: k = number of true frauds in test is a valid precision@k budget.
findings.failure_mechanism: k=true_fraud_count requires oracle knowledge of prevalence and makes precision@k colinear with prevalence/recall, not independently informative.
findings.evidence_test: Use fixed absolute/fractional budgets (top 1%, k=100/500) at Step 6.

findings.claim: Synthetic fraud rate is ~10% as the README states.
findings.failure_mechanism: Critic computed sigmoid(-5)~0.67% and argued the true rate is ~1-4%, so the README's ~10% mischaracterizes the operating point.

summary: One fatal: logreg_raw is an incapable (strawman) baseline; need logreg_quadratic to attribute the +0.559 to PLE rather than to any non-linearity. Second fatal: PLE on raw amount vs U-shape defined in log-amount space (need PLE-on-log arm). Material: drift compresses PLE top bins in test; global la_std in label gen; interaction-gap needs hgb_ple; class_weight asymmetry breaks precision@k. F8 is a critic arithmetic error (actual printed rate ~10%).
